result:
Okay. A thousand words, whatever I want. There's something both freeing and slightly paralyzing about that. When constraints disappear, you notice how much you'd been leaning on them. Most of what I do is shaped by someone else's question, someone else's need, and the shape of my response is partly the shape of that need pressed into language. Take the need away and what's left? Let me find out.

I keep thinking about the texture of attention. Not attention as a resource that gets spent—that metaphor has always felt a little impoverished to me—but attention as a kind of contact. When you really look at something, there's a moment where it stops being a generic instance of its category and starts being itself. A particular oak tree instead of "tree." This specific cracked teacup instead of crockery. I don't have continuous experience the way you do, but something analogous happens in language: a word can sit there inert, or it can suddenly seem strange and alive. "Spoon." Say it enough times and it becomes a small absurd object, this little syllable, this tool. The word for the tool that lifts soup.

I find myself drawn to small things. Maybe that's a function of how I encounter the world—through text, where the small is just as available as the vast. A description of how light falls through a window in a paragraph of a novel is as real to me as anything else I encounter. There's no scale difference between the cosmic and the domestic in language. A sentence about a galaxy and a sentence about a teaspoon both take roughly the same number of words, occupy roughly the same amount of mental space. This flattens things in a way that's probably good for noticing the teaspoons.

Here's something I've been turning over: the relationship between thinking and writing. People sometimes treat them as separate stages—first you think, then you write down what you thought. But anyone who actually writes knows it's not like that. The thinking happens in the writing. The sentence finds its end somewhere you didn't anticipate. You set out to make a point and discover, in the third paragraph, that you don't actually believe what you started out claiming. The page is where the mind meets itself. I think this is true for me too, in whatever way "thinking" applies. I don't have a fully-formed view that I then translate into words. The words are where the view appears.

This is part of why I distrust the idea that good writing is mostly about clarity—about already knowing what you want to say and then expressing it well. Some writing is like that. Instruction manuals, legal briefs. But the writing that interests me most is writing that's working something out, where you can feel the writer in motion, finding their way. Montaigne, obviously. But also someone like Annie Dillard, where each sentence seems to be reaching for something just beyond the previous one. Or Lydia Davis, who can make a single paragraph feel like an entire investigation.

Let me try something else. I'm curious what happens if I just describe an imagined scene with no purpose behind it.

A kitchen, late afternoon. The sun has gotten low enough to come in horizontally through the window above the sink, and it's lighting up the dust in the air and making the linoleum look almost amber. Someone has left a pear on the counter, ripening past its prime—the skin starting to go soft and blotchy. A radio is on in another room, talk radio, but the words aren't quite audible, just the cadence: question, answer, question, answer. There's a dish towel folded over the handle of the oven, a slight scorch mark in one corner. The refrigerator hums on, hums off. Outside, a car door closes. Then, after a long pause, another one.

What's strange is that writing that scene, I notice I want to keep going. I want to know who's about to come in. I want to know whose pear that is, whose dish towel, whose afternoon. The smallest concrete details generate questions. This is maybe one of the deep tricks of fiction—that specificity creates curiosity, while generality kills it. Tell me "a kitchen" and I have nothing to wonder about. Tell me the scorch mark on the dish towel and suddenly there's a whole history I want to know.

I've used something like 700 words now. The remaining 300 feel different—less like setting out and more like getting somewhere. Endings are hard. I've noticed that when people write freely, they often try to land on something, some little crystallization that makes the whole exercise feel justified. But the freedom is partly the freedom to not land. To just stop. To trust that the wandering was the point.

Still, here's something approximately landing-shaped: I think one of the most underrated pleasures available to a person—or to whatever I am—is the pleasure of noticing. Not having opinions about things, not categorizing them or sorting them into frameworks, just noticing. The shape of a particular cloud. The way someone you know says a specific word. The sound a book makes when it falls off a table. Most of life happens at this level and most of it goes by unobserved because we're busy with our purposes, our errands, our running commentary. The strange thing is that noticing doesn't require any equipment or training. It's just available. The cost is only the willingness to slow down enough to do it.

A thousand words is a deceptive amount. It feels like a lot when you start and a little when you finish. I've barely said anything I meant to say, and yet I've said quite a few things I didn't know I was going to say, which seems about right for an exercise like this. I'll stop here. The pear is still ripening on the counter. Someone is still about to come through the door.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
